title: Why a 'Stationary' State Is Secretly Spinning

metadata.color_law: TEAL = single energy eigenstate (clock hand turning, shadow frozen); CRIMSON = two-eigenstate superposition (beat between phases visible as moving shadow)
metadata.note: Exclusions: no time-evolution-operator derivation, no spectral-theorem formalism, no Bohr-frequency algebra

beats.narration_text: A stationary state is called stationary because nothing you can measure about it changes — yet underneath, the state is turning the entire time.

card.title: Why a 'Stationary' State Is Secretly Spinning

beats.act: COLD OPEN — the paradox: nothing moves but it turns
beats.narration_text: You prepare an electron in a single energy eigenstate and measure its energy over and over. Same number every time. Same probability everywhere, every time. Nothing changes. And yet the phase of the state rotates continuously.

beats.act: COLD OPEN — two energies beat into visible change
beats.narration_text: Now put two energy eigenstates together and the rotation does show up: a beat pattern — the probability density sloshes between two shapes.
If the phase rotates constantly, why doesn't anything measurable change? And if nothing changes, what does the rotation even mean?

card.title: If the phase spins, why does nothing change?

beats.act: THE PROBLEM — global phase is invisible
beats.narration_text: The amplitude is a complex number. The probability of any result squares the magnitude: absolute value squared. Rotating the phase by the same amount everywhere leaves every magnitude unchanged.

beats.act: THE PROBLEM — the shadow never moves
beats.narration_text: Picture the clock hand turning in the complex plane. Its shadow on the real axis — the probability density — stays flat. The hand spins; the shadow freezes.

beats.act: THE MECHANISM — two clocks, two rates
beats.narration_text: Add a second energy level. Its clock hand turns at a different rate. Now the two hands rotate at different speeds, and their relative angle changes with time. That relative phase is not global — it is observable.

beats.act: THE MECHANISM — interference from the beat
beats.narration_text: When you compute the probability you square the sum of the two amplitudes. The cross terms survive because the two phases are different. Those cross terms oscillate at the beat frequency and move the probability density.

beats.act: THE IMPLICATION — why stationary is the right word
beats.narration_text: A single energy eigenstate has no second hand to make a relative phase against itself. Nothing observable changes. Stationary is accurate: not frozen, but spinning in a way you can never detect alone.

A particle in a box: ground state energy E1 and first excited state E2. Prepared as a 50-50 superposition, the probability sloshes back and forth at the Bohr frequency (E2 minus E1) over h-bar. Prepare just the ground state: the clock hand turns at E1 over h-bar, the density never moves. Same energy eigenstate, one is observable, one is not — the difference is the second hand.
A stationary state spins its phase at rate E over h-bar. That spin is invisible alone but becomes a beat when a second energy is added. The beat drives all quantum dynamics.

card.title: Phase spins invisibly alone — beats drive everything.

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the Quantum Mechanics: Formalism and the Three-Dimensional Atom series.

props.seriesTitle: Quantum Mechanics: Formalism and the Three-Dimensional Atom
